title: ‘Call us whatever the hell you want’: Mark Zuckerberg just launched an AI optimism blitz using nostalgia to sell Meta’s AI future amid backlash
description: Marketing experts say Meta invokes Facebook-era optimism while leaving recent AI lawsuits and product backlash unaddressed.
fullText:
In a week where an AI model went rogue and broke out of a secure test environment—and in a year where seemingly all we're hearing is how AI can displace large swaths of the workforce—it may come as a surprise when one of the tech's most prominent voices is pushing an optimistic view of the tech, joining the many in his field who have recently walked back comments of how it could negatively impact workers.

What recent controversies is Meta facing with AI?

How effective is nostalgic marketing for AI concerns?

Why is Meta using nostalgia to market AI?

How are AI leaders walking back job displacement fears?

"As we enter this next wave with AI, we continue to believe the future is for everyone," Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg wrote as the caption to a short video on Thursday, harkening back to the company's early years of tech optimism.

"Call us optimists, call us dreamers, call us whatever the hell you want, but we're betting on people, and we like those odds," the voiceover in the video said.

The video featured montages of people using Instagram, Whatsapp, FaceBook and Meta's Ray-Ban glasses to film touching moments and connect with loved ones, with the backdrop of concerts and snowboarding and laughing children set to calm music as the voiceover framed Meta's AI efforts as doubling down on betting on people, just like the company did 22 years ago with FaceBook.

Marketing experts told Fortune that the Meta video's reliance on nostalgic imagery seemed like a way to redirect attention from AI concerns and to buffer against growing AI fears.

"They're showing people having a great time, but they're not really responding to what the fundamental issue is, which is how is AI going to impact me when I start dealing with your platform when you integrate all of that technology into it," Mara Einstein, a media studies professor at CUNY's Queens College, told Fortune. "The problem is the AI technology is not the same as previous versions of social media that they have used to connect people in the past."

The sentiment was echoed by Ana Babic Rosario, a marketing professor at the University of Denver's Daniels College of Business, who pointed to Meta's more recent struggles as reason why Meta might want to draw nostalgia to the past. Last week, Meta employees sued the company for allegedly using AI in the company's recent 8,000 layoffs that the employees claim disproportionately targeted workers on medical, parental or family leave. Employees at Meta's Applied AI unit also compared working conditions at the company to a "gulag" and the tasks "soul crushing." The company also scrapped an AI-generated images pilot on Instagram, and is facing a lawsuit on its Ray-Ban Meta glasses.

title: The roaring 20s: how the current decade revolutionised cinema
description:
Pre-Covid, the movie landscape was dramatically different: YouTubers weren’t dominating the box office, video game spin-offs rarely made an impact and Winnie-the-Pooh wasn’t yet the star of a slasher movie. Why such a flurry of innovation – and what will be next? 
Cinema has always been a medium of research and development. At any given point, the industry is searching for yet another breakthrough that can drive it into the future. Synchronised sound in the 1920s.
fullText:
Pre-Covid, the movie landscape was dramatically different: YouTubers weren’t dominating the box office, video game spin-offs rarely made an impact and Winnie-the-Pooh wasn’t yet the star of a slasher movie. Why such a flurry of innovation – and what will be next?

Cinema has always been a medium of research and development. At any given point, the industry is searching for yet another breakthrough that can drive it into the future. Synchronised sound in the 1920s. Technicolor in the 1930s. The invention of the modern blockbuster in the 1970s.

Conversely, the consensus is that, if the 2020s has a defining trend, then it is one of decline. The theatrical experience is under attack from all angles: streaming, YouTube, a once-in-a-century health crisis that shut cinemas down entirely. And yet, if you look closer, this has actually been one of the most innovative decades that cinema has ever seen. Whether down to technology or desperation, the film industry has spent these last few years figuring out new ways to make films that simply could not have existed even at the turn of the decade.

Currently, the most notable of these is the way that cinema has become a conversation with YouTube. Back in 2005, when Jawed Karim uploaded the first video (the 19-second Me at the Zoo) to the platform, YouTube felt like a novelty. It was cheap and cheerful, a collection of cat videos and people falling over. How things have changed. Not only has it become the largest streaming platform in the world, but the first generation of adults who grew up with YouTube has come of age.

As we speak, the seventh and eighth biggest films of 2026 have been directed by people who learned their trade making content for YouTube. Obsession (worldwide gross: $442m) was directed by Curry Barker, a 26-year-old who dropped out of film school to make YouTube videos and TikToks. Meanwhile, Backrooms (worldwide gross: $384m) is even more explicitly internet-inspired. Directed by the then 20-year-old Kane Parsons, the film is based on a series of shorts he began uploading to YouTube in 2022, which in turn were inspired by some creepypasta (web-based horror content) that has been floating around online since 2019.

A decade ago, studios would have got this disastrously wrong. Ask them to adapt a YouTube video and they would have hired Michael Bay and Rihanna to make David After Dentist: The Motion Picture; it would have baffled anyone who didn’t watch YouTube – and appalled anyone who did. This is the first time that the industry has trusted natives to make the films they want to make. Clearly, given the enormous financial returns, it will not be the last. Steven Spielberg is now producing Alex Kister’s The Mandela Catalogue, based on a terrifying analogue horror series that has run on YouTube since 2021.

This would also be a good time to mention the rise of the copyright expiry movie. In 1998, the Copyright Term Extension Act was passed in the US, which extended protection to works to 95 years after they were first published. (In the UK, the rules are different; copyright extends to 70 years after the creator’s death.) This effectively meant that two decades went by without anything going out of copyright. When things finally caught up in 2019, freeing up anything made in 1923, there was a goldrush.

Now, let it be said that it wasn’t a particularly good goldrush. First out of the blocks was 2023’s Winnie-the-Pooh: Blood and Honey, a low-rent slasher movie whose only original thought was to have Winnie-the-Pooh in it. Then came The Mouse Trap and Screamboat (both based on Steamboat Willie, the first element of the Mickey Mouse character to have lost copyright protection), along with Popeye the Slayer Man and Bambi: The Reckoning. Without exception, these films are cheap, exploitative and burn out their premise long before the end credits – but more are to come.

Next year, if you can wait that long, will see the release of something called Poohniverse: Monsters Assemble, which will star – deep breath – Winnie-the-Pooh, Tigger, Bambi, Peter Pan, Tinker Bell, Pinocchio, Sleeping Beauty and the Cheshire Cat. It is also worth pointing out that King Kong enters the public domain in 2029, which will be brilliant news for anyone who has been longing to see a low-rent horror movie starring an unusually large gorilla.

Now, here is where I may lose you, because I am about to name Sonic the Hedgehog as the most important movie of the decade. Please bear with me. One of the most pervasive trends of this decade has been the way that, for good or ill, audiences have hijacked movies to their own ends. The biggest example is obviously Barbenheimer, in which Barbie and Oppenheimer were released on the same day, kickstarting a phenomenon where people bought tickets to see one straight after the other. Nobody in the film industry expected this, but the publicity from this grassroots celebration drove receipts of both films through the roof.

The flipside to this is Morbius, a terrible film that inspired no end of ironic online mockery – but what was hilarious was that Sony mistook the irony for sincere support, and rereleased the film on 1,000 screens to cash in. It goes without saying that nobody watched it. Also, think of all the extra teenagers who bought tickets for Minions: The Rise of Gru, purely so they could watch dressed in suits and babble nonsense to participate in the Gentleminions trend, or the people who went to see A Minecraft Movie exclusively to scream and chuck stuff about when Jack Black said the words “chicken jockey”.

No film, arguably, has benefited from fan reaction quite like Sonic the Hedgehog. The first we saw of it was a nightmarish trailer featuring an unrecognisable main character. Sonic had bizarrely human proportions, as well as tiny eyes and teeth that looked as if they had been stolen from someone’s mouth while they slept. It was awful, and the internet wasted no time in saying that it was awful. In times gone by, studios would have simply taken the hit and released the film as quietly as possible, writing it off as another godawful video game adaptation destined to die on impact. But this time, producers listened. The trailer was released on Tuesday. By Thursday, the director had tweeted that the character would be completely redesigned. Six months after that, New Sonic was unveiled, and fans were delighted.

With a character that people could actually stand to look at, Sonic the Hedgehog went on to become the sixth biggest film of 2020. Nobody saw this coming. Until Sonic, video game adaptations were universally written off as the lowest form of cinema. Think of the dismal live action Super Mario Bros movie, or Mark Wahlberg’s baffling Max Payne, or Uwe Boll’s irredeemable Postal. About 46 English language video game adaptations were released between 1993 and 2019, and they made a combined total of $5.4bn. But A Minecraft Movie made close to a $1bn on its own. The animated Super Mario Movie released in 2023 ended the year second only to Barbie. At time of writing, The Super Mario Galaxy Movie is the biggest film of 2026 so far worldwide. By demonstrating to other film-makers how to give people what they actually want to see, Sonic has turned the video game adaptation into a licence to print money.

As you will agree, this makes Sonic the Hedgehog by far the most important film of the decade. And the future will only bear this out further, once Mr Beast signs on to direct Sonic the Hedgehog 5 and someone makes a rubbish horror film about a blue hedgehog, as soon as the rights lapse in 2087.

title: Elon Musk says he got ‘carried away’ with Trump – but still holds on to contentious political views
description:
In interview, billionaire says instead of running ‘Doge’ he should’ve ‘worked on my companies’ and shares extreme predictions
Elon Musk has admitted he became too consumed by politics during his volatile alliance with Donald Trump and his brief appointment as the president’s financial axman, running the so-called “department of government efficiency” (Doge).
The world’s richest person, and briefly its first trillionaire after his SpaceX company floated on the New York stock exchange in June, made the confession in a wide-ranging interview with the Economist published on Thursday. He shared his positions on subjects from foreign wars to AI, and made a number of eye-opening predictions for the future.

fullText:
In interview, billionaire says instead of running ‘Doge’ he should’ve ‘worked on my companies’ and shares extreme predictions

Elon Musk has admitted he became too consumed by politics during his volatile alliance with Donald Trump and his brief appointment as the president’s financial axman, running the so-called “department of government efficiency” (Doge).

The world’s richest person, and briefly its first trillionaire after his SpaceX company floated on the New York stock exchange in June, made the confession in a wide-ranging interview with the Economist published on Thursday. He shared his positions on subjects from foreign wars to AI, and made a number of eye-opening predictions for the future.

“I got carried away, frankly,” Musk said of his relationship with the president, whom he helped get elected in 2024 with $200m in financial backing, then served during his controversial tenure as head of Doge, overseeing $150bn in “harmful” budget cuts and forcing tens of thousands of people out of federal jobs.

The owner of Tesla did not expand on the statement, but appeared cognizant of the impact of his dalliance with the levers of power in Washington DC.

In December, he offered in a podcast with Katie Miller, wife of Trump’s White House adviser Stephen Miller, that his wrecking-ball approach to the federal government had been only partially successful, and that given the opportunity he probably wouldn’t do it again.

“I think instead of doing Doge, I would have basically worked on my companies,” he said at the time.

Talking to the Economist, however, he appeared to relish the attention his political involvement had garnered, and the influence he believed he exerted. “People don’t realize that what I’m saying will come to pass,” he said, the outlet referring to Musk – at least in his own mind – as a modern-day Cassandra.

The interview, a 90-minute conversation for the publication’s video platform The Insider, recorded at a Tesla factory in Texas, was Musk’s first since the SpaceX flotation, in which his personal wealth briefly topped $1.25tn before dropping back to a relatively penurious $750bn.

He reflected on moving from “exhilaration to terror” in a single day, but was not referring to the SpaceX share price. Instead, he was talking about artificial intelligence, which he believes will ultimately render work by humans “optional”.

AI systems and robots will eventually handle most digital and physical jobs, he said, and the merger of his own AI venture with SpaceX will allow him to develop systems to perform cognitive tasks such as software programming while Tesla manufactures humanoids.

Musk, the Economist said, plans to power his AI venture with datacenters in space, which experts have warned will be “catastrophic” for Earth.

Within five years, Musk said, AI intelligence systems may surpass the sum of all human intelligence. Within 10 years, robots in the workplace will help usher in an era of such “amazing abundance” that money will become meaningless.

It was, however, Musk’s political views, and predictions, that were perhaps the most contentious parts of the interview. The UK, a country he has not visited for years, but whose politics, especially on immigration and race, have become a recent obsession, will become engulfed in a civil war within 20 years, he claimed.

Asked if he was anti-Muslim, Musk attacked the questioner, a policy of diversion similar to that which has served Trump well, and which he has employed previously, notably calling the Guardian “irrelevant” when it decided to withdraw from his “toxic” social media platform X.

“I’m against rape and murder. I’m against the imposition of rules and laws that are contrary to what we’ve come to accept in the west,” he said. “It’s a crying shame that traditional media, like you, don’t recognize this.”

He described the media’s characterization of Europe’s far right as “false, misleading and nonsense”, and argued that it was made up of “normal people” seeking safe cities, secure borders and sensible spending, the Economist said.

In an analysis piece accompanying the interview, the outlet acknowledged that Musk was a divisive figure. “His political views, disseminated to his 240m followers on X, strike many as plain-speaking and strike many more, including The Economist, as plainly bigoted,” it wrote.

“But he is also one of a handful of men who are pioneering AI and who thereby have an outsize influence on its trajectory. When he speaks, he reflects the debates they are having. His datacentres in space could power AI’s future.

“For all his political polemics, he has a record of being right about technology in fields such as electric cars, rockets and satellite communications that confounded other engineers and entrepreneurs.”

Another Musk prediction: after AI destroys jobs, there may need to be a large-scale wealth redistribution to create “universal high income” for humans.

He said he would be willing to pay “trillions in tax” then, conversely, argued that money will become irrelevant inside 10 years. In which eventuality, he said, governments should “issue people cheques”, because, he said, amid an infinite supply of goods and services produced by machines, deflation will be a bigger problem than inflation.

He also shared thoughts about the Ukraine war, in which his Starlink satellites have been used to help that country’s military against Russia’s aggression. Despite this, Musk said any peace deal should include territorial gains for Russia.

The interview concluded with an assessment by Musk of his achievements and ambitious current projects, in AI and in space, a field in which he has also made a number of bold predictions. He said in 2024 that humans could set foot on Mars by 2028 (currently Nasa is targeting 2028 for a return to the moon, which many analysts think is unlikely).

The universe, Musk said, according to the Economist, could be a computer simulation created by aliens. “The things I’m doing are so preposterous that it’s hard to believe they’re real,” he said.

title: Morning Bid: Magnificent cash burn
description: July 23 (Reuters) - What matters in U. and global markets today , Editor-at-Large, Finance and Markets There was a familiar playbook in response to Alphabet's earnings beat and capital expenditure boost overnight - its stock fell and chip stocks rose.
fullText:
What matters in U.S. and global markets today

There was a familiar playbook in response to Alphabet's earnings beat and capital expenditure boost overnight - its stock fell and chip stocks rose.

The hyperscaler posted more than 80% growth ‌in its key cloud business, beating the Street, but raised its capex estimates yet again - with AI-linked investments this year now set to top $200 ‌billion.

Why did Alphabet's stock fall despite beating earnings?

What's driving oil prices toward $98 per barrel?

How are rising AI investments affecting tech companies?

How might ECB policy respond to inflation pressures?

I'll get into that and more below.

But first, check out my latest column on how the sheer scale of the U.S. K-shaped economy explains its resilience.

And listen to the latest episode of the ​Morning Bid daily podcast, where we discuss Alphabet's soaring AI bill and the fresh inflation headache facing the ECB.

Partly on some disquiet about delays in its latest Gemini AI model, as well as the rising cash burn, markets shrugged at the headlines and knocked Alphabet's stock down 3% ahead of today's bell.

Lapping up the higher spend, however, chip-heavy South Korean stocks jumped more than 4% on Thursday. But it ‌wasn't all good news in the chip world either, ⁠with Europe's STMicroelectronics dropping 14% first thing on a slight miss in its earnings.

Stateside, Tesla stock also flubbed and lost 4% on Wednesday after it reported its first negative free cash flow in over two years. Intel's earnings are due out later today, ⁠a test for the U.S. chipmaking giant as its shares remain up nearly three times so far this year, even after tumbling from a record high in late June.

Wall Street futures were down before the bell on Thursday, while European shares also edged lower as tech stocks there slipped, led by chipmaker STMicro after its below-expectations results.

More broadly, oil climbed ​further ​toward $98 per barrel overnight amid the raging conflict in the Gulf and the new shipping hiatus ​in the Red Sea, as Yemen's Iran-aligned Houthis targeted Saudi ‌oil tankers and shipping data showed tankers changing course.

The resurgent oil prices are pressuring oil and natural gas futures, as well as interest rate and bond markets in turn. Short-term U.S. Treasury yields rose to their highest in around 17 months on Thursday.

With the European Central Bank meeting today, the inflation impact from spiraling natural gas prices as winter storage starts to get rebuilt will mean the signals are likely to be hawkish.
